---
title: "Alex Event Ledger CSV — User Manual"
subtitle: "How to read alex_event_ledger_ref9_plus_targets5.csv"
author: "Austin Li"
date: "2026-05-07"
date-format: "long"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    code-overflow: wrap
---

```{=html}
<style>
:root {
  --alex-ink: #172033;
  --alex-muted: #637083;
  --alex-blue: #2f66b3;
  --alex-green: #2d8053;
  --alex-amber: #b66e1f;
  --alex-border: #d7dee9;
  --alex-panel: #fbfcff;
}
body { color: var(--alex-ink); background: #f6f8fb; }
.quarto-title-block .title { font-weight: 800; letter-spacing: 0; }
.quarto-title-block .subtitle { color: var(--alex-muted); }
.callout-note {
  border-left: 4px solid var(--alex-blue);
  background: #f7fbff;
  border-radius: 0 8px 8px 0;
}
.callout-warning {
  border-left: 4px solid var(--alex-amber);
  background: #fffaf0;
  border-radius: 0 8px 8px 0;
}
figure {
  margin: 2rem auto;
  padding: 1rem;
  background: #fff;
  border: 1px solid var(--alex-border);
  border-radius: 8px;
  box-shadow: 0 2px 8px rgba(23, 32, 51, 0.05);
  box-sizing: border-box;
}
.quarto-figure:has(> figure.quarto-float-fig) {
  width: min(1040px, calc(100vw - 19rem));
  max-width: calc(100vw - 2rem);
  margin-left: 0;
  margin-right: auto;
}
.quarto-figure > figure.quarto-float-fig {
  width: 100% !important;
  max-width: 100%;
  margin-left: 0;
  margin-right: 0;
  overflow-x: auto;
}
.quarto-float-tbl {
  max-width: 100%;
  overflow-x: auto;
}
figure img {
  display: block;
  width: 100%;
  max-width: 100%;
  height: auto;
  margin: 0 auto;
}
figcaption { color: var(--alex-muted); font-size: 0.92rem; }
table { font-size: 0.92em; border: 1px solid var(--alex-border); }
thead th { background-color: #eef3f8; color: var(--alex-ink); }
tbody tr:nth-child(even) { background: var(--alex-panel); }
code { color: #243247; }
td, th { overflow-wrap: anywhere; }
.code-row {
  font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', Menlo, monospace;
  font-size: 0.85em;
  background: #f8fafc;
  padding: 0.75em 1em;
  border: 1px solid var(--alex-border);
  border-left: 4px solid var(--alex-blue);
  border-radius: 8px;
  margin: 0.75em 0;
  white-space: pre-wrap;
}
@media (max-width: 700px) {
  .quarto-figure:has(> figure.quarto-float-fig),
  .quarto-figure > figure.quarto-float-fig {
    width: 100%;
    max-width: 100%;
    margin-left: auto;
    margin-right: auto;
    padding: 0.75rem;
  }
  .quarto-figure > figure.quarto-float-fig img {
    width: 760px;
    max-width: none;
  }
  table { display: block; max-width: 100%; overflow-x: auto; }
  colgroup, col { width: auto !important; }
  pre.sourceCode code,
  pre.sourceCode code > span { white-space: pre-wrap; overflow-wrap: anywhere; }
}
</style>
```

::: {.callout-note title="What this manual covers"}
This is the column-by-column and code-by-code reference for the file
`output/review_csv/alex_event_ledger_ref9_plus_targets5.csv`. Every
coded value in the file is defined here, with at least one worked
example pulled directly from that file. The companion document
**`pipeline_overview.html`** explains how the file is generated and how
the underlying pipeline works.
:::

# What this CSV is

`alex_event_ledger_ref9_plus_targets5.csv` is a deterministic
spreadsheet view of the M&A bidding process for 14 deals: 9 reference
deals (verified against their SEC filings) and 5 target deals (extracted
but pre-verification). It is generated by

```bash
python scripts/export_alex_event_ledger.py --scope all \
    --output output/review_csv/alex_event_ledger_ref9_plus_targets5.csv
```

which reads the latest trusted graph snapshots in
`output/extractions/{slug}.json` and projects them through
`pipeline/deal_graph/project_alex_event_ledger.py`. There is no LLM
call in this step. Re-running the command on unchanged snapshots
produces a byte-identical CSV.

## File at a glance

| | |
|---|---|
| **Path** | `output/review_csv/alex_event_ledger_ref9_plus_targets5.csv` |
| **Total rows** (excluding header) | 419 |
| **Columns** | 28 |
| **Distinct deals** | 14 (9 reference + 5 target) |
| **All review burden zero?** | yes — `needs_human_attention=0` for every row |
| **Confidence breakdown** | `high`: 396 rows, `medium`: 23 rows, `low`: 0 rows |

## What one row represents

Each row is **one event, one relation, or one participation count**. It
is **not**:

- one row per bidder (consortiums are preserved as single bidders)
- one row per claim (multiple claims may collapse into one canonical
  event)
- one row per filing page (a filing page typically contributes many rows)
- a price-projection ledger (no `bI`, `bF`, `T`, `admitted` —
  those fields are reserved for downstream econometric tooling and are
  not in this file)

Within each deal, rows are sorted by `event_date`, then by a fixed
event-priority map (advisor first, contacts, NDAs, IOIs, bids,
advancements, dropouts, exclusivity, financing commitments, merger
execution; participation counts come last). The `event_order` column
encodes that order in multiples of 10.

## The deal slate in this snapshot {#sec-deal-slate}

```{=html}
<table class="table"><caption>Table: 14 deals in the current ledger. Reference 9 carry filing-grounded verification reports; target 5 are post-gate-release but have no individual verification reports yet.</caption>
<thead><tr><th>Slug</th><th>Target</th><th>Acquirer</th><th>Announced</th><th>Rows</th><th>Verified?</th></tr></thead>
<tbody>
<tr><td><code>imprivata</code></td><td>IMPRIVATA INC</td><td>(per filing)</td><td>2016</td><td>31</td><td>✓</td></tr>
<tr><td><code>mac-gray</code></td><td>MAC GRAY CORP</td><td>CSC SERVICEWORKS, INC.</td><td>2013-10-14</td><td>37</td><td>✓</td></tr>
<tr><td><code>medivation</code></td><td>MEDIVATION INC</td><td>(per filing)</td><td>2016</td><td>20</td><td>✓</td></tr>
<tr><td><code>penford</code></td><td>PENFORD CORP</td><td>(per filing)</td><td>2014–15</td><td>28</td><td>✓</td></tr>
<tr><td><code>petsmart-inc</code></td><td>PETSMART INC</td><td>BC Partners + Caisse + GIC + StepStone + Longview (Buyer Group)</td><td>2014-12-14</td><td>33</td><td>✓</td></tr>
<tr><td><code>providence-worcester</code></td><td>PROVIDENCE &amp; WORCESTER RR CO</td><td>GENESEE &amp; WYOMING INC</td><td>2016-08-15</td><td>39</td><td>✓</td></tr>
<tr><td><code>saks</code></td><td>SAKS INC</td><td>HUDSON'S BAY COMPANY</td><td>2013-07-29</td><td>33</td><td>✓</td></tr>
<tr><td><code>stec</code></td><td>STEC INC</td><td>(per filing)</td><td>2013</td><td>42</td><td>✓</td></tr>
<tr><td><code>zep</code></td><td>ZEP INC</td><td>NEW MOUNTAIN CAPITAL</td><td>2015-04-08</td><td>38</td><td>✓</td></tr>
<tr><td><code>art-technology-group-inc</code></td><td>ART TECHNOLOGY GROUP INC</td><td>(per filing)</td><td>2010–11</td><td>23</td><td>—</td></tr>
<tr><td><code>gen-probe-inc-new</code></td><td>GEN PROBE INC NEW</td><td>HOLOGIC INC</td><td>2012-04-30</td><td>33</td><td>—</td></tr>
<tr><td><code>m-g-c-diagnostics-corp</code></td><td>M G C DIAGNOSTICS CORP</td><td>(per filing)</td><td>2013</td><td>16</td><td>—</td></tr>
<tr><td><code>multimedia-games-holding-co-inc</code></td><td>MULTIMEDIA GAMES HOLDING CO INC</td><td>(per filing)</td><td>2013–14</td><td>31</td><td>—</td></tr>
<tr><td><code>wafergen-bio-systems-inc</code></td><td>WAFERGEN BIO SYSTEMS INC</td><td>TAKARA BIO INC</td><td>2014–15</td><td>15</td><td>—</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Trust posture"}
Reference deals (✓) have a filing-grounded verification report at
`quality_reports/reference_verification/{slug}.md` confirming exact
quote binding for sample claims. Target deals (—) have passed graph
integrity checks (`status=passed_clean`) but have **no individual
verification report yet**. For target deals, treat the ledger as a
*candidate* representation of the auction process, not a certified one.
:::

# Reading a single row end-to-end {#sec-walkthrough}

To anchor the column-by-column reference that follows, here is one row
read in full. We pick `providence-worcester:40`, which is a typical bid
submission with all the bid-specific fields populated.

::: {.code-row}
deal_slug=providence-worcester  TargetName=PROVIDENCE & WORCESTER RR CO  Acquirer=GENESEE & WYOMING INC  DateAnnounced=2016-08-15
event_order=40  event_date=2016-07-12  date_precision=exact
row_unit=bidder  party_name=Party C  bidder_class=strategic
initiated_by=bidder  event_family=bid  event_code=bid_submitted  stage=initial  formality=informal
dropout_side=not_applicable  dropout_reason=not_applicable
bid_value=21.0  bid_value_lower=  bid_value_upper=  bid_value_unit=per_share  consideration_type=unspecified
review_label="Party C submitted initial bid (21.0)"
evidence_page=36
evidence_quote_full="On July 12, 2016, Party C submitted an IOI with an offer price per share of $21.00"
confidence=high  needs_human_attention=0
source_claim_ids=claim_da42b5669b149876
:::

Walking left to right:

- `deal_slug = providence-worcester`. The lowercase identifier of the
  deal; matches the directory under `data/filings/`.
- `TargetName / Acquirer / DateAnnounced`. Pulled from
  `data/filings/{slug}/manifest.json` via `state/progress.json`. Same
  for every row of a given deal.
- `event_order = 40`. The 4th row of this deal in chronological / priority
  order. Multiples of 10 leave room for inserts on review without
  reflowing the whole sheet.
- `event_date = 2016-07-12`, `date_precision = exact`. The filing
  states a precise date; the date column is in ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` form
  (see @sec-date-precision).
- `row_unit = bidder`. The party_name is a single named/anonymised
  bidder, not a count or a deal-level placeholder.
- `party_name = Party C`. The filing's anonymous handle for this
  bidder.
- `bidder_class = strategic`. The LLM emitted (or downstream
  canonicalisation derived) a strategic class; see @sec-bidder-class.
- `initiated_by = bidder`. The evidence quote contains "submitted",
  which is one of the bidder-verb keywords (see @sec-initiated-by).
- `event_family = bid`, `event_code = bid_submitted`, `stage =
  initial`, `formality = informal`. The IOI is treated as a
  first-round-bid event; first-round bids are informal by convention
  in the projection map.
- `bid_value = 21.0`, `bid_value_unit = per_share`,
  `consideration_type = unspecified`. The filing supports a $21.00
  per-share figure but does not state cash-vs-stock here, hence
  unspecified consideration.
- `evidence_page = 36`. The filing page where the quote was bound.
- `evidence_quote_full = "On July 12, 2016, Party C submitted an IOI
  with an offer price per share of $21.00"`. The byte-exact filing
  substring. No paraphrasing.
- `confidence = high`. The LLM was confident; the quote tightly
  supports every typed field of the claim.
- `needs_human_attention = 0`. The row is clean; nothing flagged.
- `source_claim_ids = claim_da42b5669b149876`. The deterministic ID of
  the claim that produced this row. Searchable in
  `output/extractions/providence-worcester.json` and the audit
  `deal_graph_v2.json`.

# Column reference

The columns appear in the CSV in the order below. The order is fixed by
`ALEX_EVENT_LEDGER_FIELDS` in `pipeline/deal_graph/project_alex_event_ledger.py`.

| # | Column | Type | What it is |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `deal_slug` | string | Lowercase deal identifier. |
| 2 | `TargetName` | string | Target company. From the filing manifest. |
| 3 | `Acquirer` | string | Final acquirer (or buyer-group label). From `state/progress.json`. May be empty for some deals. |
| 4 | `DateAnnounced` | string (ISO date or empty) | Public announcement date. |
| 5 | `event_order` | int | Multiple of 10 within a deal; row position after sorting. |
| 6 | `event_date` | string (ISO date or empty) | When the event occurred. Empty if the filing did not state a date. |
| 7 | `date_precision` | enum | `exact` / `unknown`. (See @sec-date-precision.) |
| 8 | `row_unit` | enum | `bidder` / `cohort` / `deal`. (See @sec-row-unit.) |
| 9 | `party_name` | string | The actor or cohort name; for `row_unit=deal`, the target company name. |
| 10 | `bidder_class` | enum | `financial` / `strategic` / `mixed` / `unknown` / `not_applicable`. (See @sec-bidder-class.) |
| 11 | `initiated_by` | enum | `bidder` / `target` / `advisor` / `activist_shareholder` / `unknown` / `not_applicable`. (See @sec-initiated-by.) |
| 12 | `event_family` | enum | High-level grouping; one of 11 values. (See @sec-event-family.) |
| 13 | `event_code` | enum | The 24 codes that drive interpretation. (See @sec-event-code.) |
| 14 | `stage` | enum | Process stage at which the event occurred. (See @sec-stage.) |
| 15 | `formality` | enum | `formal` / `informal` / `not_applicable`. (See @sec-formality.) |
| 16 | `dropout_side` | enum | `bidder` / `target` / `no_response` / `mixed_or_unknown` / `not_applicable`. (See @sec-dropout-side.) |
| 17 | `dropout_reason` | enum | 8 values. Derived from quote keywords. (See @sec-dropout-reason.) |
| 18 | `bid_value` | number or empty | Point value when filing supports a single number. |
| 19 | `bid_value_lower` | number or empty | Range lower bound when filing supports a range. |
| 20 | `bid_value_upper` | number or empty | Range upper bound. |
| 21 | `bid_value_unit` | enum | `per_share` / `enterprise_value` / `equity_value` / `unspecified` / `not_applicable`. (See @sec-bid-unit.) |
| 22 | `consideration_type` | enum | `cash` / `stock` / `mixed` / `other` / `unspecified` / `not_applicable`. (See @sec-consideration.) |
| 23 | `review_label` | string | Human-readable summary auto-generated from event_code + party. |
| 24 | `evidence_page` | string | Filing page(s) where the bound quote(s) live; pipe-delimited if multiple refs. |
| 25 | `evidence_quote_full` | string | Byte-exact filing substring(s); pipe-delimited if multiple refs. |
| 26 | `confidence` | enum | `high` / `medium` / `low`. The LLM's self-reported confidence. |
| 27 | `needs_human_attention` | int | `1` if low-confidence, missing quote, or non-clean review status; else `0`. |
| 28 | `source_claim_ids` | string | Deterministic ID of the underlying claim. |

In the next section, every coded column gets its own enum reference
with worked-example rows.

# Coded values: exhaustive reference {#sec-codes}

Notation: each worked-example header is `slug:event_order`; the example
is the actual row in the CSV. The identifying numbers are the values you
search on if you want to confirm a row by hand.

## row_unit {#sec-row-unit}

`row_unit` says **what kind of party the row is about**. There are
three values; each has a clear physical meaning.

| Value | Meaning | Count in this CSV |
|---|---|---|
| `bidder` | A specific named or handle-anonymised bidder, advisor, holder, or other identifiable actor. | 328 |
| `cohort` | A count of unnamed parties (e.g. "11 strategic buyers"), or a group-membership row. | 88 |
| `deal` | The row is about the deal/process itself, not an individual party (rare; used for deal-level events that have no specific actor). | 3 |

::: {.callout-note title="When the projection chooses each"}
The projection in `project_alex_event_ledger.py` uses `_row_unit_for_event`:

- if the event's actor is a `cohort` actor or the label contains a
  cohort word ("parties", "bidders", "buyers", "sponsors",
  "companies", "remaining") → `cohort`
- else if the event has a named actor → `bidder`
- else → `deal`
:::

**Worked examples:**

- `row_unit = bidder` → `providence-worcester:40` ("Party C submitted
  initial bid").
- `row_unit = cohort` → `providence-worcester:140` (`initial_contact`
  for "11 potential strategic buyers and 18 potential financial
  buyers").
- `row_unit = deal` → `zep:200` (`go_shop_ended`, party_name = "ZEP
  INC"). Used because a go-shop window ending has no specific
  acting bidder; it is a deal-level event.

## bidder_class {#sec-bidder-class}

`bidder_class` is the **only** classification of bidders we record.
Geographic (US/non-US) and public/private flags are explicitly
**out of scope** per `rules/bidders.md`.

| Value | Meaning | Count |
|---|---|---|
| `financial` | Financial sponsor / private-equity firm / financial-buyer cohort. | 103 |
| `strategic` | Operating or corporate buyer / strategic-buyer cohort. | 155 |
| `mixed` | Filing supports a mixed financial/strategic bidder, group, or cohort. | 16 |
| `unknown` | Filing does not support classifying the actor. | 91 |
| `not_applicable` | Row is not about a bidder (e.g. an advisor relation). | 54 |

The class is "locked" once supported by an actor claim. Group classes
are refined by walking member relations: a group with both a strategic
and a financial member resolves to `strategic` by deliberate convention
(`pipeline/deal_graph/canonicalize.py`, `_propagate_member_class_signals`).

**Worked examples:**

- `strategic` → `providence-worcester:40`. Party C is a strategic
  rail-industry buyer per the filing context.
- `financial` → `zep:170`. New Mountain Capital is a PE sponsor.
- `mixed` → `imprivata:270`. Cohort row "15 mixed parties"; the
  underlying participation count was filed at the mixed level.
- `unknown` → `providence-worcester:140`. The cohort "11 potential
  strategic buyers and 18 potential financial buyers" is a single
  contact event for both classes; the row's class is `unknown` because
  the cohort itself spans two classes.
- `not_applicable` → `providence-worcester:10`. Advisor row (GHF as
  the Company's banker); the column does not apply to advisor
  relations.

## initiated_by {#sec-initiated-by}

For `event_family = contact` and `bid` only. Other rows are
`not_applicable`. Detection logic is keyword-based and runs in the
projection (`_initiated_by` in
`pipeline/deal_graph/project_alex_event_ledger.py`).

| Value | Meaning | How detected |
|---|---|---|
| `bidder` | Bidder approached the target, submitted unsolicited, or otherwise self-initiated. | Quote contains one of: "unsolicited", "submitted", "delivered", "made a proposal", "made an offer", "offered", "approached". |
| `target` | Target's board / management / committee initiated the contact. | Quote contains "authorized" / "instructed" / "directed" + "contact" / "solicit", **or** matches a target-contact phrase like "the company contacted", "the board contacted", "committee contacted", "management contacted", "reached out to". |
| `advisor` | Advisor (banker, lawyer) made the contact. | Quote contains "contacted" + "representatives" without target-side initiation phrases. |
| `activist_shareholder` | Activist or holder pressure surfaced in the quote. | Quote contains "activist". |
| `unknown` | None of the patterns matched on a `contact_initial` event. | Default for contact rows. |
| `not_applicable` | Event family is not `contact` or `bid`. | Everything else. |

**Worked examples:**

- `bidder` → `providence-worcester:30`. "Party C submitted an IOI" →
  "submitted" → bidder.
- `target` → `petsmart-inc:210`. "the board authorized management to
  contact Industry Participant" → "authorized" + "contact" → target.
- `advisor` → `providence-worcester:140`. "representatives of GHF
  contacted 11 potential strategic buyers" → "contacted" +
  "representatives" → advisor.
- `unknown` → `providence-worcester:150`. "met with one of the
  Company's Class I rail partners ('Party A') to discuss joint
  commercial opportunities" — no clear initiator keyword.
- `not_applicable` → `providence-worcester:10` (advisor retained;
  outside `contact`/`bid` families).

::: {.callout-warning title="Reviewer note"}
This column is the most rule-of-thumb in the ledger. The detection
logic is a regex-style keyword check, not a semantic parse. If a
quote's plain reading contradicts the column, that is a calibration
finding worth noting.
:::

## event_family {#sec-event-family}

Eleven groupings. The mapping from canonical event subtypes / relation
types to family is fixed in `EVENT_SUBTYPE_MAP` and `RELATION_TYPE_MAP`.

```{=html}
<table class="table"><caption>Table: 11 event_family values, with the codes that map to each.</caption>
<thead><tr><th>Family</th><th>Member event_codes</th><th>Count</th><th>Example row</th></tr></thead>
<tbody>
<tr><td><code>contact</code></td><td><code>initial_contact</code></td><td>31</td><td><code>providence-worcester:140</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>process</code></td><td><code>nda_signed</code>, <code>participation_count</code></td><td>100</td><td><code>providence-worcester:160</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>bid</code></td><td><code>ioi_submitted</code>, <code>bid_submitted</code></td><td>121</td><td><code>providence-worcester:40</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>advisor</code></td><td><code>advisor_retained</code></td><td>51</td><td><code>providence-worcester:10</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>agreement</code></td><td><code>exclusivity_granted</code>, <code>merger_agreement_executed</code></td><td>21</td><td><code>zep:170</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>dropout</code></td><td><code>bidder_withdrew</code>, <code>target_excluded</code>, <code>no_response</code>, <code>cohort_closed</code></td><td>51</td><td><code>providence-worcester:100</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>advancement</code></td><td><code>advanced</code>, <code>not_advanced</code></td><td>12</td><td><code>providence-worcester:260</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>financing</code></td><td><code>financing_relation</code>, <code>financing_committed</code></td><td>13</td><td><code>petsmart-inc:70</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>support</code></td><td><code>voting_support</code>, <code>support_agreement</code>, <code>rollover_support</code></td><td>10</td><td><code>petsmart-inc:180</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>consortium</code></td><td><code>consortium_ca_signed</code>, <code>joined_group</code>, <code>exited_group</code></td><td>6</td><td><code>saks:280</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>go_shop</code></td><td><code>go_shop_started</code>, <code>go_shop_ended</code></td><td>3</td><td><code>zep:190</code></td></tr>
</tbody></table>
```

## event_code {#sec-event-code}

This is **the most important column** — it carries the substance of
each row. There are 24 distinct codes in the current CSV. Each is
defined by the projection map (`EVENT_SUBTYPE_MAP` / `RELATION_TYPE_MAP`)
in `pipeline/deal_graph/project_alex_event_ledger.py`. Codes 1–18 come
from event subtypes; codes 19–23 come from actor relations; code 24
comes from participation count claims.

Every code below is followed by a worked-example row from the actual
CSV. The format is **slug:event_order**; the *quote* shown is the
verbatim `evidence_quote_full` from that row.

### Process & advisor codes

**`advisor_retained`** — A target or bidder engaged a financial /
legal advisor. Source: `actor_relation_claim` with `relation_type =
advises`. Stage: `process_setup`. Family: `advisor`. Count: **51**.

> `providence-worcester:10` — party `GHF`. Quote: *"the subcommittee
> recommended to the Board that the Company retain GHF as its
> investment banking firm | the Board approved the subcommittee's
> recommendation and the Company engaged GHF"*.

**`initial_contact`** — First contact between a potential bidder and
the target / advisors. Source: `event_subtype = contact_initial`.
Stage: `pre_bid`. Family: `contact`. Count: **31**.

> `providence-worcester:140` — party `11 potential strategic buyers
> and 18 potential financial buyers`. Quote: *"During the week of
> March 28, 2016, in accordance with the Transaction Committee's
> directives, representatives of GHF contacted 11 potential
> strategic buyers (including Party A) and 18 potential financial
> buyers."*

**`nda_signed`** — Target-bidder confidentiality agreement executed.
Source: `event_subtype = nda_signed`. Stage: `nda`. Count: **36**.
Distinct from `consortium_ca_signed`, which is a bidder-bidder
agreement.

> `providence-worcester:160` — party `Party C`. Quote: *"After
> executing a confidentiality agreement, Party C was provided the
> memorandum concerning the Company provided to the other potential
> buyers."*

**`consortium_ca_signed`** — Bidder-bidder or intra-consortium
confidentiality agreement. Does *not* substitute for a target-side
NDA. Stage: `nda`. Family: `consortium`. Count: **2**.

### Bid codes

**`ioi_submitted`** — Indication of interest submitted (typically
informal, no binding terms). Source: `event_subtype = ioi_submitted`.
Stage: `initial`. Formality: `informal`. Count: **11**.

> `providence-worcester:30` — party `Party C`. Quote: *"On July 12,
> 2016, Party C submitted an IOI with an offer price per share of
> $21.00"*.

**`bid_submitted`** — A formal bid (first-round, revised, or
final-round) submitted. The `stage` column tells you which round.
Sources: `event_subtype` ∈ {`first_round_bid`, `final_round_bid`} and
also bid claims with `bid_stage` set. Count: **110** (the largest
single code).

> `providence-worcester:40` — party `Party C`, stage = `initial`,
> bid_value = 21.0 per_share. Quote: *"On July 12, 2016, Party C
> submitted an IOI with an offer price per share of $21.00"*. (Note
> that the same quote supports both `ioi_submitted` and
> `bid_submitted` rows; the projection emits both because the IOI
> contained a price.)

> `providence-worcester:60` — party `G&W`, stage = `revised`,
> bid_value = 21.15. (Same deal, later round.)

**`participation_count`** — A row that reports a count, not a single
party. Source: `participation_count_claim`. Family: `process`.
Stage: the claim's `process_stage`. Count: **64**.

> `providence-worcester:340` — party `11 strategic parties`,
> stage = `contacted`. Quote: *"representatives of GHF contacted 11
> potential strategic buyers (including Party A)"*.

### Advancement codes

**`advanced`** — Bidder admitted into the next round / due-diligence
phase. Source: `event_subtype = advancement_admitted`. Stage:
`advancement`. Count: **10**.

> `providence-worcester:260` — party `G&W and Party B`. Quote:
> *"The Transaction Committee concluded that the Company should
> proceed with confirmatory due diligence and negotiations with G&W
> and Party B"*.

**`not_advanced`** — Bidder not invited to advance. Source:
`event_subtype = advancement_declined`. Count: **2**.

> `gen-probe-inc-new:230` — party `Party C`. Quote: *"representatives
> of Morgan Stanley contacted representatives of Party C to encourage
> the submission of a bid. After deliberations, Party C declined to
> make an offer."*

### Dropout codes

**`bidder_withdrew`** — Bidder voluntarily withdrew. Stage: `dropout`.
`dropout_side = bidder`. Count: **28**.

> `providence-worcester:100` — party `Party E`. Quote: *"Party E
> withdrew its revised proposal on August 2, 2016 (but confirmed its
> original proposal of $21.26 per share)"*.

**`target_excluded`** — Target removed bidder from the process.
`dropout_side = target`. Count: **9**.

> `providence-worcester:280` — party `remaining bidders` (cohort).
> Quote: *"representatives of GHF subsequently contacted the
> remaining bidders to inform them that they were no longer involved
> in the process"*.

**`no_response`** — Filing affirmatively states a bidder did not
respond / proceed. `dropout_side = no_response`. Count: **12**.

> `providence-worcester:310` — party `one strategic buyer and one
> financial buyer`. Quote: *"One strategic buyer and one financial
> buyer elected not to submit an LOI."*

**`cohort_closed`** — An anonymous cohort ended participation as a
unit (e.g. when the filing collapses two bidders into one handle).
`dropout_side = mixed_or_unknown`. Count: **2**.

> `petsmart-inc:250` — party `Bidder 3`. Quote: *"the ad hoc
> committee authorized these two bidders to work together. We refer
> to these two bidders together as 'Bidder 3.'"*

### Agreement codes

**`exclusivity_granted`** — Target grants exclusive negotiation
rights to one bidder. Family: `agreement`. Count: **7**.

> `zep:170` — party `New Mountain Capital`. Quote: *"our board of
> directors authorized an extension of exclusivity with New Mountain
> Capital through April 7, 2015, which extension letter was signed on
> March 18, 2015."*

**`merger_agreement_executed`** — The transaction agreement was
signed. Stage: `execution`. Count: **14**.

> `providence-worcester:320` — party `G&W`. Quote: *"the Company and
> G&W executed the merger agreement"*.

### Financing & support codes

**`financing_relation`** — A financing tie between two parties (e.g.
a sponsor backing a strategic bidder). Source: `actor_relation_claim`
with `relation_type = finances`. Family: `financing`. Stage:
`financing`. Count: **7**.

> `providence-worcester:90` — party `Party F`, financing party `Party
> E`. Quote: *"Party E submitted a revised LOI, along with financing
> support, from Party F, at a price of $23.81 per share"*.

**`financing_committed`** — Buyer's financing sources committed
(typically alongside merger-agreement signing). Source:
`event_subtype = financing_committed`. Count: **6**.

> `petsmart-inc:70` — party `Buyer Group`. Quote: *"On December 6,
> 2014, the Buyer Group and Bidder 2 submitted their respective
> comments on the draft merger agreement and other transaction
> documents, and the Buyer Group provided its financing commitment
> documents."*

**`voting_support`** — A holder agrees to vote in favour of the
transaction. Source: `actor_relation_claim` with `relation_type =
voting_support_for`. Family: `support`. Count: **6**.

> `petsmart-inc:180` — subject `Longview`, object `merger`. Quote:
> *"Longview's commitment to vote for the merger | On December 14,
> 2014, the parties executed the merger agreement, the voting
> agreement and related transaction agreements"*.

**`support_agreement`** — Generic support / cooperation relationship.
`relation_type = supports`. Count: **2**.

**`rollover_support`** — A holder rolls equity into the surviving
entity. `relation_type = rollover_holder_for`. Count: **2**.

### Consortium codes

**`joined_group`** — An actor joined a buyer group. Family:
`consortium`. Stage: `consortium`. `row_unit = cohort`. Count: **2**.

> `saks:280` — subject `Sponsor A`, object `Sponsor A and Sponsor E`.
> Quote: *"Sponsor E would again be joined in its proposal by Sponsor
> A as a primary participant"*.

**`exited_group`** — An actor left a buyer group. Count: **2**.

> `saks:270` — subject `Sponsor A`, object `Sponsor A and Sponsor E`.
> Quote: *"Sponsor A and Sponsor E, who were considering participating
> in a potential joint acquisition of Saks | Sponsor A was no longer
> intending to be a primary participant in a potential transaction"*.

### Go-shop codes

**`go_shop_started`** — Post-signing market-check window opens.
Source: `event_subtype = go_shop_started`. Count: **1** in this
snapshot.

> `zep:190` — party `BofA Merrill Lynch` (the advisor running the
> go-shop). Quote: *"Representatives of BofA Merrill Lynch commenced
> the go-shop process on our behalf on April 8, 2015."*

**`go_shop_ended`** — Window closes. Count: **2**.

> `zep:200` — party `ZEP INC` (`row_unit = deal`). Quote: *"the
> 'go-shop period' that began on the date of the merger agreement and
> continued until 11:59 p.m., New York City time, on May 7, 2015"*.

## stage {#sec-stage}

Process stage at which the event occurred. There are 19 distinct
values in the current CSV. Most are pinned by the projection's
subtype map; a handful come directly from the bid claim's `bid_stage`.

```{=html}
<table class="table">
<thead><tr><th>Stage</th><th>Meaning</th><th>Count</th><th>Example row</th></tr></thead>
<tbody>
<tr><td><code>process_setup</code></td><td>Advisor retention, very early process steps.</td><td>51</td><td><code>providence-worcester:10</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>pre_bid</code></td><td>Initial outreach before any bids.</td><td>31</td><td><code>providence-worcester:140</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>contacted</code></td><td>Participation count for parties contacted.</td><td>30</td><td><code>providence-worcester:340</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>nda</code></td><td>Confidentiality phase (target-side NDAs).</td><td>38</td><td><code>providence-worcester:160</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>nda_signed</code></td><td>Participation count for NDAs signed.</td><td>16</td><td><code>providence-worcester:350</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>consortium</code></td><td>Inter-bidder agreements / membership changes.</td><td>4</td><td><code>saks:270</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>initial</code></td><td>First-round bids and IOIs.</td><td>60</td><td><code>providence-worcester:30</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>ioi_submitted</code></td><td>Participation count for IOIs.</td><td>10</td><td><code>providence-worcester:390</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>first_round</code></td><td>Participation count for first-round bids.</td><td>3</td><td><code>zep:370</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>revised</code></td><td>Revised bid round.</td><td>29</td><td><code>providence-worcester:60</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>final_round</code></td><td>Best-and-final round.</td><td>27</td><td><code>providence-worcester:250</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>advancement</code></td><td>Round-progression decisions.</td><td>12</td><td><code>providence-worcester:260</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>dropout</code></td><td>Exits from the process.</td><td>51</td><td><code>providence-worcester:100</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>exclusivity</code></td><td>Exclusive negotiation period.</td><td>8</td><td><code>zep:150</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>financing</code></td><td>Financing relationships and commitments.</td><td>13</td><td><code>providence-worcester:90</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>support</code></td><td>Voting / rollover / support agreements.</td><td>10</td><td><code>petsmart-inc:180</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>execution</code></td><td>Merger agreement signing.</td><td>14</td><td><code>providence-worcester:320</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>go_shop</code></td><td>Post-signing market check.</td><td>3</td><td><code>zep:190</code></td></tr>
<tr><td><code>unspecified</code></td><td>Bid stage not specified by filing.</td><td>9</td><td><code>providence-worcester:50</code></td></tr>
</tbody></table>
```

## formality {#sec-formality}

Distinguishes binding/structured submissions from preliminary
indications. Mapping is fixed in the projection's subtype map.

| Value | Meaning | When set | Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| `informal` | IOI or first-round bid; preliminary, non-binding. | `ioi_submitted`, `first_round_bid`, plus first-round bids that include a price range. | 98 |
| `formal` | Final-round bid (best-and-final). | `final_round_bid` and bid claims with `bid_stage=final`. | 23 |
| `not_applicable` | Row is not a bid (advisor relations, NDAs, dropouts, agreements, etc.). | Everything else. | 298 |

**Worked examples:**

- `informal` → `providence-worcester:30` (IOI; "submitted an IOI with
  an offer price per share of $21.00").
- `formal` → `providence-worcester:250` (final-round bid; stage =
  `final_round`).
- `not_applicable` → `providence-worcester:10` (advisor retained).

## dropout_side {#sec-dropout-side}

Who caused / experienced the dropout. Set by the projection's subtype
map for `event_family = dropout` rows; `not_applicable` everywhere
else.

| Value | Meaning | Count |
|---|---|---|
| `bidder` | Bidder withdrew voluntarily. | 28 |
| `target` | Target excluded the bidder. | 9 |
| `no_response` | Bidder did not respond / proceed. | 12 |
| `mixed_or_unknown` | Cohort closure where the side cannot be cleanly attributed. | 2 |
| `not_applicable` | Not a dropout event. | 368 |

## dropout_reason {#sec-dropout-reason}

For dropout rows only. The projection runs a keyword check on the
evidence quote (`_dropout_reason` in
`pipeline/deal_graph/project_alex_event_ledger.py`). The first matching
keyword wins; falls back to `unknown` when none match.

| Value | Detected when quote contains | Count | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `below_prior_bid` | "below" + ("prior" \| "earlier" \| "indication") | 1 | `imprivata:150` |
| `below_market` | "market value" or "market price" | 1 | `multimedia-games-holding-co-inc:60` |
| `strategic_fit` | "strategic fit" | 3 | `imprivata:40` |
| `financing` | "financ" (substring) | 1 | `providence-worcester:310` |
| `valuation` | "valuation" \| "price" \| "premium" | 4 | `mac-gray:220` |
| `confidentiality` | "confidential" or "leak" | 1 | `imprivata:90` |
| `unknown` | none of the above | 40 | `providence-worcester:100` |
| `not_applicable` | not a dropout | 368 | most rows |

**Worked examples:**

- `below_prior_bid` → `imprivata:150`. Quote: *"Sponsor B had stated
  that its final bid, if it were to submit one, would be significantly
  lower that its previous indication of interest. | The Special
  Committee determined that it would not be in the …"*
- `below_market` → `multimedia-games-holding-co-inc:60`. Quote: *"On
  August 28, 2013, Bidder C informed us that it was not willing to
  make an offer above the market price of our common stock …"*
- `strategic_fit` → `imprivata:40`. Quote: *"… an acquisition of the
  Company would not be a strategic fit for it, and therefore it was
  no longer interested …"*
- `financing` → `providence-worcester:310` (the cohort no-response
  row above; the substring "financ" appears via "financial buyer").
  *Note this is a noisy hit — the quote describes the cohort, not a
  financing rationale, but the keyword detector cannot tell.*
- `valuation` → `mac-gray:220`. Quote: *"Party C did not submit a
  revised indication of interest or reiterate its prior indication of
  interest of an all-cash purchase price of $16.00 to $17.00 per share
  …"* (substring "price" matched).
- `confidentiality` → `imprivata:90`. Quote: *"Strategic 3 had
  executed a confidentiality agreement and met with management, it
  did not submit a bid by the June 9, 2016 deadline"*.
- `unknown` → `providence-worcester:100`. Withdraw quote contains no
  rationale keyword.

::: {.callout-warning title="Reviewer note on dropout_reason"}
Because this column is keyword-based, false positives are common and
**this is the column most likely to need calibration**. The
`providence-worcester:310` example above is a real false-positive
hit. Consider any non-`unknown`, non-`not_applicable` value as a
*candidate* reason that should be confirmed against the quote, not
as a final attribution.
:::

## bid_value_unit {#sec-bid-unit}

Tells you how to interpret `bid_value` / `bid_value_lower` /
`bid_value_upper`. The projection sets `not_applicable` for any
non-bid row.

| Value | Meaning | Count |
|---|---|---|
| `per_share` | Number is a price per share. | 95 |
| `enterprise_value` | Number is an aggregate enterprise transaction price. | 2 |
| `equity_value` | Number is an aggregate equity transaction price. | 0 in this snapshot |
| `unspecified` | Filing supports a number but does not state which basis. | 1 |
| `not_applicable` | Not a bid row, or bid row with no value. | 321 |

**Worked examples:**

- `per_share` → `providence-worcester:130` (G&W, $25.00, all-cash).
- `enterprise_value` → `m-g-c-diagnostics-corp:60` (Altus,
  $40,000,000, consideration unspecified).
- `unspecified` → `saks:110` (Company H, $2,600,000,000 — a number
  is supported, but the filing wording does not clearly fix it as
  enterprise vs equity).
- `not_applicable` → almost every non-bid row, e.g.
  `providence-worcester:10`.

::: {.callout-note title="Why we don't collapse to per-share"}
Earlier versions of this ledger collapsed all values to per-share.
That was retired (see `rules/bids.md`): aggregate values cannot be
faithfully converted without share-count assumptions, and labelling
an aggregate as per-share would silently corrupt downstream
analysis. The current ledger preserves the unit the filing uses.
:::

## consideration_type {#sec-consideration}

What the offer would have paid in. Bid rows only; everything else
is `not_applicable`.

| Value | Meaning | Count |
|---|---|---|
| `cash` | All cash. | 50 |
| `stock` | All stock. | 1 |
| `mixed` | Mix of cash, stock, CVRs, etc. | 10 |
| `other` | Non-monetary or unusual consideration. | 1 |
| `unspecified` | Bid emitted but the filing did not state the consideration. | 59 |
| `not_applicable` | Not a bid row. | 298 |

**Worked examples:**

- `cash` → `providence-worcester:130`. G&W's $25.00 all-cash bid.
- `stock` → `wafergen-bio-systems-inc:130`. "Company X" — stock-only
  consideration.
- `mixed` → `providence-worcester:50`. G&W's $21.15 per-share with
  mixed consideration (cash plus other).
- `other` → `wafergen-bio-systems-inc:30`. Takara Bio — non-cash,
  non-stock structure.
- `unspecified` → `providence-worcester:40`. Party C's IOI — price
  given but not the cash/stock split.
- `not_applicable` → any non-bid row.

## date_precision {#sec-date-precision}

Computed by `_date_precision` in the projection: a regex on
`event_date`.

| Value | Detected when event_date matches | Count in this CSV |
|---|---|---|
| `exact` | `YYYY-MM-DD` | 238 |
| `month` | `YYYY-MM` | 0 |
| `year` | `YYYY` | 0 |
| `rough` | non-empty string that matches none of the above | 0 |
| `unknown` | empty | 181 |

In the current snapshot every dated event is dated to the day; the
month/year/rough buckets exist for future cases where the filing pins
down only a vaguer time.

## confidence {#sec-confidence}

The LLM's self-reported confidence on the underlying claim. From
`rules/schema.md`: `high` / `medium` / `low`.

| Value | Count | What it should mean |
|---|---|---|
| `high` | 396 | The cited quote tightly supports every typed field. |
| `medium` | 23 | One or more typed fields require a small inferential step from the quote. |
| `low` | 0 in this snapshot | Filing supports the claim only weakly; usually paired with a precise quote. |

**Medium-confidence examples** (worth a closer look):

- `providence-worcester:330` (`voting_support`). Quote: *"the Company,
  G&W and the Eder Trusts executed the voting agreement"* — the
  trustee-as-voter inference is not in the quote literally.
- `medivation:190` (`nda_signed`). Quote: *"entered into
  confidentiality agreements with several parties, including
  Sanofi"* — supports an NDA in spirit, but "several parties" is a
  count, not a single named NDA.
- `medivation:200` (`participation_count`). Same quote — the LLM read
  the same sentence as a count.
- `imprivata:150` (`target_excluded`). Quote concerns Sponsor B's own
  signal that its bid would be lower; the *exclusion* attribution is
  inferential.

## needs_human_attention {#sec-attention}

Binary flag set by `_needs_human_attention`:

```python
if review_status not in ("", "clean"):  return 1
if confidence == "low":                  return 1
if not bound_quote and not supplied_quote: return 1
return 0
```

In this snapshot the value is **0 for every row** — no row is currently
flagged for attention. The mechanism still matters: if any of the three
conditions above started firing in a future run, the corresponding
rows would surface `needs_human_attention=1` and the deal's status
would shift from `passed_clean` toward `needs_review`.

# How to spot anomalies

Recommended review heuristics, ordered by leverage:

1. **`needs_human_attention = 1`**. Sort the CSV on this column
   descending. Anything flagged is the first place to look. (In the
   current snapshot: empty.)
2. **`confidence = medium` or `low`**. Filter and read every quote.
   The 23 medium rows in this CSV are listed in @sec-confidence.
3. **`dropout_reason ∉ {unknown, not_applicable}`**. The keyword
   detector is approximate. Any non-trivial dropout reason should be
   confirmed against the quote. Particularly: `valuation` and
   `financing` are the easiest false positives (any quote mentioning
   "price" or any cohort containing "financial" can hit them).
4. **`bid_value_unit = unspecified` on a row that has a `bid_value`**.
   Two rows in this snapshot:
   `saks:110` (Company H, $2.6 billion). Confirm against the filing
   page whether the figure is enterprise or equity value.
5. **`row_unit = cohort` rows**. These represent unnamed counts.
   Cross-check the count and class against the sentence quoted —
   especially when a cohort spans multiple classes (the
   `providence-worcester:140` cohort row sets `bidder_class=unknown`
   for that reason; you may want a finer attribution).
6. **`consortium` family rows**. The filing's bidding unit drives
   actor identity. If a member appears as its own bidder elsewhere in
   the CSV without a clear filing basis for separate bidding conduct,
   that is a calibration finding.
7. **`merger_agreement_executed` not the *last* event for a deal**.
   The standard story is "agreement → optional go-shop → done". If a
   bid or contact event appears *after* `merger_agreement_executed`,
   the projection priority probably failed in an unusual filing
   structure; worth a look.
8. **`evidence_page` mismatch with the deal's Background section**.
   The Background section spans a known page range per filing (for
   example, mac-gray pp. 33–47). If a row cites a page well outside
   the section, the citation unit binding chose a wrong paragraph.

# What this CSV is *not*

- **Not the canonical truth.** The canonical graph for any deal is
  `output/extractions/{slug}.json` and the run-specific
  `output/audit/{slug}/runs/{run_id}/deal_graph.duckdb`. The CSV is a
  projection of those.
- **Not a one-row-per-bidder ledger.** This shape was deliberately
  retired (see commit `3e2f40f Purge stale extraction contracts`).
  Old workflows that expected a fixed bidder-row schema will not work.
- **Not a price-projection sheet.** The Python-owned post-review
  fields (`bI`, `bI_lo`, `bI_hi`, `bF`, `admitted`,
  `formal_boundary`, `dropout_mechanism`, `T`) are not in this CSV.
  They are reserved for downstream econometric tooling.
- **Not Alex's 2026 workbook.** The legacy workbook is calibration
  material. When the filing and the workbook disagree, the filing
  wins. (`AGENTS.md`, "Source of Truth" section.)

# AI-assisted review path {#sec-ai-review}

The CSV is the lowest-friction view, but for structural questions —
"every dropout in petsmart-inc with no specified reason", "all
financial sponsors who later pulled financing for a strategic" — a
spreadsheet is awkward. The pipeline produces two richer artefacts
that are designed for AI-mediated review.

![Choosing the right review artefact. The CSV is best for spreadsheet
scanning, JSONL for AI-assisted row review, DuckDB for structural joins, and
pages.json for quote disputes.](assets/artefact_picker.svg){#fig-artefact-pick
fig-alt="Flowchart from a review question to CSV for spot checks, review JSONL
for cross-row AI review, DuckDB graph for joins, and pages.json for filing
quote verification."}

Artefact paths (referenced in the diagram):

- **JSONL review rows** → `output/review_rows/{slug}.jsonl`
- **DuckDB graph file** → `output/audit/{slug}/runs/{run_id}/deal_graph.duckdb`
- **`pages.json`** → `data/filings/{slug}/pages.json`

## Option 1: Claude Desktop on the JSONL

The simplest AI path: drop
`output/review_rows/{slug}.jsonl` (or several together) into a Claude
Desktop conversation. The JSONL files have **richer fields** than the
CSV — every review row carries `bound_source_quote`,
`bound_source_page`, `supplied_quote` (the LLM's verbatim quote),
`issue_codes`, `suggested_action`, the canonical claim ID, the
deal/run pair, and so on.

Sample prompts that work well:

- *"In petsmart-inc, list every `bid_submitted` with its bound page
  and bid_value. Group by bidder."*
- *"For mac-gray, show the dropout chain in chronological order with
  one line per dropout: who, when, why."*
- *"Across all 9 reference deals, find every row where
  `confidence=medium` and explain why the quote does not tightly
  support the typed fields."*
- *"For zep, list every actor relation involving New Mountain Capital
  and its objects."*

## Option 2: Claude Code on the repository

If you can spare 5 minutes for setup, point Claude Code (the CLI
equivalent) at the repo root. It can read every artefact —
`pages.json`, `deal_graph_v2.json`, `state/progress.json`,
`state/flags.jsonl` — and connect them to each other. It can also run
shell commands, which means it can query DuckDB directly.

Sample prompts:

- *"Open `output/audit/mac-gray/runs/{latest}/deal_graph.duckdb` and
  list every actor with `bidder_class=unknown` plus the events
  involving each."*
- *"Compare the `event_subtype` distribution across the three saks
  runs in the stability proof; tell me what changed."*
- *"For petsmart-inc, find the Background section of `pages.json`,
  pull out the paragraphs cited by `evidence_page` 32–34, and check
  whether the bound quotes are accurate."*

## Option 3: DuckDB CLI directly

For users comfortable with SQL, the per-run DuckDB file is queryable
without any AI:

```bash
duckdb output/audit/petsmart-inc/runs/{run_id}/deal_graph.duckdb
```

Sample queries:

```sql
-- All bidders and their classes for one deal:
SELECT actor_label, actor_kind, bidder_class FROM actors;

-- All events in chronological order with the bidding actor:
SELECT e.event_subtype, e.event_date, l.actor_label
FROM events e LEFT JOIN event_actor_links l USING (event_id)
ORDER BY e.event_date NULLS LAST;

-- Participation counts by stage:
SELECT process_stage, actor_class, count_min, count_max, count_qualifier
FROM participation_counts;

-- Claims with a non-supported disposition (review-only):
SELECT c.claim_id, c.claim_type, d.disposition, d.reason
FROM claims c JOIN claim_dispositions d USING (claim_id)
WHERE d.disposition <> 'supported' AND d.current = true;
```

The schema is in `pipeline/deal_graph/store.py` and contains roughly
25 tables — actors, actor_relations, events, bids (as events),
participation_counts, claims, claim_dispositions, claim_evidence,
evidence, row_evidence, event_actor_links, plus a few bookkeeping
tables for coverage obligations.

## Which to use when

| Question | Recommended artefact |
|---|---|
| "Sort the rows by date, see the whole picture" | CSV |
| "Filter to one event_code, look at quotes" | CSV |
| "Cross-deal structural question, no joins" | JSONL via Claude Desktop |
| "Verify quotes against the actual filing" | Claude Code (it can read `pages.json`) |
| "Join actors with their relations and events" | DuckDB (CLI or AI-assisted) |
| "Find a row by `source_claim_ids`" | `output/extractions/{slug}.json` |

# Glossary

Quick reference for the codes and column names used here. The
pipeline-side glossary (claim, evidence, disposition, run ID, etc.)
is in **`pipeline_overview.html`**.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `advised party` | The actor receiving advice in an `advises` relation (typically the target or the bidder). |
| `bidder_class` | The actor's financial / strategic / mixed / unknown classification. **No** geographic or public/private flags. |
| `Buyer Group` | A consortium treated by the filing as a single bidder unit. |
| `cohort` | A count of unnamed parties; the filing names a count, not individual names. |
| `consortium` | Multi-member bidding unit; preserved as one actor in the canonical graph. |
| `event_code` | The 24-value column that drives interpretation; see @sec-event-code. |
| `event_family` | High-level event grouping (11 values); see @sec-event-family. |
| `event_order` | Multiple of 10 within a deal; row position after sorting. |
| `evidence_page` | Filing page where the bound quote lives. Pipe-delimited if multiple refs. |
| `evidence_quote_full` | Byte-exact filing substring(s); pipe-delimited if multiple refs. |
| `formality` | `formal` (final-round / signed) vs `informal` (IOI / first-round) vs `not_applicable`. |
| `IOI` | Indication of interest. Preliminary, non-binding submission. |
| `LOI` | Letter of intent (a stronger, often binding, submission). The CSV codes LOIs as `bid_submitted`. |
| `participation_count` | A row that reports a count of unnamed parties at a process stage. |
| `process_stage` | For participation counts: `contacted` / `nda_signed` / `ioi_submitted` / `first_round` / `final_round` / `exclusivity`. |
| `review burden` | Number of rows in `output/review_rows/{slug}.jsonl` whose `review_status` is not `clean`. Determines `passed_clean` (0) / `needs_review` (1–10) / `high_burden` (>10). |
| `row_unit` | What kind of party the row is about: `bidder` / `cohort` / `deal`. |
| `source_claim_ids` | Deterministic ID of the underlying claim; searchable in `output/extractions/{slug}.json`. |

---

::: {.callout-note title="Companion document"}
For *how* the CSV is produced — the LLM call, quote binding,
canonicalisation, validation, the verification gate — see
**`pipeline_overview.html`**.
:::
